title: A Reichkanzler is Taking Shape Along Pennsylvania Avenue
summary: "So we now have a situation whereby the intimidating Mr. Trump has injected two right-wing ideologues (Bannon and Flynn) into the national security mix, and between the three of them, they probably figure they can carry the day on most issues. Pardon my observation that it sounds like 1933 in the ancient fatherhood of the Drumpf (sic) family."
body: In 19th Century politics, and with German reunification, the title of Reichkanzler (Reich Chancellor) was considered equivalent to Prime Minister in other Western countries. One could almost pin that tag on Donald Trump after he upended the National Security Council (NSC) on January 28. Two members of the NSC were invited to leave, to be called back only when a subject involving them was to be discussed. These are the Director of National Intelligence (formerly the Director of Central Intelligence) and the chairman of he Joint Chiefs of Staff. In a way this is defensible, as the DNI, as an intelligence officer, is not supposed to be involved in policy discussions; and the JCS Chairman is not in the chain of command (which runs from the President to the Secretary of Defense to the military commanders in the field). From the beginning, the only two statutory members of the NSC have been the Secretary of State and the Secretary of Defense. However, in an executive order on January 28, Mr. Trump named Stephen K. Bannon a member of the NSC. Bannon, a former naval officer and head of the right-wing website Breitbart, is a White House adviser of Mr. Trump and does not need to be confirmed by the Senate. Neither does the National Security Adviser, Gen. Michael Flynn. So we now have a situation whereby the intimidating Mr. Trump has injected two right-wing ideologues (Bannon and Flynn) into the national security mix, and between the three of them, they probably figure they can carry the day on most issues. Pardon my observation that it sounds like 1933 in the ancient fatherhood of the Drumpf (sic) family. Recommended citation Cogan, Charles G. "A Reichkanzler is Taking Shape Along Pennsylvania Avenue." _list.source: Belfer Center for Science and International Affairs, Harvard Kennedy School

result.main_content_en: The article analyzes President Donald Trump's restructuring of the National Security Council (NSC) in January 2017. It notes that Trump removed the Director of National Intelligence and the Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff as regular attendees, a move with some procedural justification. However, the significant change was the appointment of his political adviser, Stephen Bannon, as a member of the NSC. Alongside National Security Adviser Michael Flynn, this inserted two right-wing ideologues into the core national security decision-making body. The author draws a historical parallel, suggesting this concentration of power around the president and his close, unconfirmed advisers resembles the rise of a 'Reich Chancellor' and evokes ominous historical precedents from 1933.
result.main_content_zh: 本文分析了特朗普总统于2017年1月对国家安​​全委员会（NSC）的重组。文章指出，特朗普将国家情报总监和参谋长联席会议主席从NSC的常设与会者中移除，这一做法在程序上存在一定的辩护理由。然而，关键的变化在于他任命其政治顾问史蒂夫·班农为NSC成员。班农与国家安全顾问迈克尔·弗林一同，将两位右翼意识形态人士注入了国家安全决策核心圈。作者认为，这一安排使得特朗普、班农和弗林三人可能在大多数议题上主导决策。文章对此提出了尖锐批评，将其与历史上的“帝国总理”权力集中模式相类比，并暗示这种局面令人联想到1933年的不祥先例，形成了总统身边由未经参议院确认的亲密顾问主导决策的局面。
